narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some academic areas, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 44.3% chance of failing the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of students' academic records, family backgrounds, and exam outcomes, aiming to identify those likely to pass on the first attempt. The model's call hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, with your undergraduate GPA emerging as the strongest positive influence, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar due to its above-average strength. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1—well below the average of about 5.5—had the next biggest impact, pulling significantly in the direction of failure risk by signaling weaker performance relative to peers that year. Your LSAT score of 29.5, notably lower than the average 36.7, also played a notable role, nudging the outlook toward potential failure despite not being the top influence. Compared to typical students, your full-time enrollment plans align with the vast majority (over 92%), and your middle family income bracket is common, so they didn't sway the decision much. These top three elements—undergrad GPA helping, but Year 3 decile and LSAT hindering—outweighed other details like your first-year decile, creating an overall risk profile that concerned admissions. It's understandable to feel frustrated, especially given your strong undergrad record as a Black male navigating these paths where representation and support can make a difference. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, as that could flip similar models in your favor next time. Strengthening recent academic deciles through targeted prep or additional coursework might also help demonstrate upward momentum. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback on holistic factors they value beyond this prediction, and explore programs with strong bar prep support tailored to diverse students like yourself. With focused improvements, you're positioned to reapply stronger and turn this into a stepping stone.
